facts.title: A divided peninsula
facts.fact: Since 1948 the peninsula has been divided into the Republic of Korea (South) and the DPRK (North), separated by the DMZ near the 38th parallel.
facts.korean_en: Korea is divided into south and north.
facts.note: South Korea's official name is **대한민국**; the DMZ truce line is the 휴전선.

facts.title: A young, lively democracy
facts.fact: South Korea is a democratic republic with a directly elected president. Its democracy is young — direct presidential elections returned only in 1987 — and turnout is famously high.
facts.note: 투표를 하다 = "to vote" (literally "to do a vote"). Many nouns pair with **하다** to form verbs.

facts.title: The Taegukgi flag
facts.fact: South Korea's flag is the **태극기**. Its red-and-blue center circle (태극) represents balance and harmony, and the four black trigrams in the corners stand for heaven, earth, fire, and water.
facts.korean_en: The Taegukgi is Korea's national flag.
facts.note: The white background symbolizes peace and purity. **태극기** literally means "taegeuk flag."

facts.title: Mugunghwa, the national flower
facts.fact: The national flower is the **무궁화** (rose of Sharon). Its name suggests "eternal" blooming, and the flower appears in the national anthem and on official emblems.
facts.note: Korea's fastest train, the **무궁화호**, is named after this flower.

facts.title: The National Assembly
facts.fact: Laws are made by the **국회** (National Assembly), a single-chamber legislature. Its members are called 국회의원, and they are chosen in general elections.
facts.korean_en: Laws are made in the National Assembly.
facts.note: 국회 = "national meeting." The building sits on **여의도** island in Seoul.

facts.title: One five-year term
facts.fact: The South Korean president serves a **single five-year term** and cannot be re-elected. This single-term rule was set to prevent any one leader from holding power too long.
facts.korean_en: The president works for five years.
facts.note: 동안 marks a span of time: **오 년 동안** = "for five years."

facts.title: Provinces and cities
facts.fact: The country is organized into provinces (**도**) and major cities. Larger units are divided further into 시 (cities), 군 (counties), and 구 (districts).
facts.korean_en: Korea has several provinces and cities.
facts.note: Examples include **경기도** (Gyeonggi Province) and 부산시 (Busan City).

facts.title: A member of the UN
facts.fact: South Korea joined the **United Nations** in 1991 and takes an active global role. A Korean diplomat, Ban Ki-moon, served as UN Secretary-General from 2007 to 2016.
facts.korean_en: Korea is a member of the United Nations.

facts.title: Local elections
facts.fact: Every four years citizens vote in **local elections** to choose mayors, provincial governors, and council members. These elections decide who runs cities and regions.
facts.korean_en: Local elections are held every four years.

facts.title: The Constitution
facts.fact: The **헌법** (Constitution) is the country's highest law. The current constitution dates from 1987, and a special Constitutional Court (헌법재판소) decides whether laws follow it.
facts.korean_en: The constitution is the most important law.
facts.note: 가장 forms the superlative: **가장 중요한** = "the most important."

facts.title: Seoul, the capital
facts.fact: **서울** is the capital and largest city, home to the presidential office and the National Assembly. Roughly half the country's population lives in the wider Seoul metropolitan area.
facts.note: 수도 means "capital city," a different word from 물 ("water"). **서울** is a pure native-Korean name.

facts.title: Sejong, the administrative city
facts.fact: **세종특별자치시** (Sejong City) was built to host many government ministries and ease congestion in Seoul. It is named after King Sejong, who created the Korean alphabet.
facts.korean_en: There are many government buildings in Sejong City.
facts.note: 많이 is the adverb "a lot"; the adjective form is **많다** ("to be many").

facts.title: Candlelight gatherings
facts.fact: South Korea has a tradition of large, peaceful **candlelight gatherings** (촛불집회), where citizens hold candles in public squares to express their views. They are known for being orderly and nonviolent.
facts.korean_en: People hold candles in the square.
facts.note: 촛불 = 초 ("candle") + 불 ("fire/light"), with a linking ㅅ (사이시옷).

facts.title: Aegukga, the anthem
facts.fact: The national anthem is **애국가**, meaning "the song of love for the country." Its lyrics describe Korea's mountains and seas and express devotion to the nation.
facts.note: 애국가 = 애국 ("patriotism") + 가 ("song"). The verb 부르다 is irregular: 불러요 in casual speech.

facts.title: Three branches of power
facts.fact: Government power is split among three branches: the executive led by the president, the **국회** (legislature), and the courts (법원). This separation keeps any one branch from holding all the power.
facts.korean_en: Korea's government is divided into three parts.
facts.note: 세 is the native number used before counters; **세 부분** = "three parts."

facts.title: Political parties
facts.fact: South Korea has several **political parties** (정당) that compete in elections. Citizens choose candidates from different parties when they vote.
facts.korean_en: There are several political parties in Korea.
facts.note: 뽑다 means "to pick/choose" and is often used for electing people: **대통령을 뽑다** = "to elect a president."

facts.title: Other national symbols
facts.fact: Beyond the flag and flower, South Korea has other emblems: the national bird is often said to be the magpie (까치), and the Korean tiger (호랑이) is a beloved symbol used as a mascot, including at the 1988 Seoul Olympics.
facts.korean_en: The tiger is a symbol of Korea.
facts.note: The peninsula's shape is sometimes likened to a **호랑이** standing upright.
